summary: My father, Thong Tran, was born in Bien Hoa, Vietnam, which is where our family roots come from. It's a place that holds a lot of history for us, especially since my father grew up there before the Vietnam War. Even though he left Bien Hoa to come to the United States in 1985, the culture and traditions from that place have stayed with us through the food and stories he shares. Bien Hoa is more than just a birthplace; it's a connection to my Vietnamese heritage that I cherish deeply.

The Vietnam War had a huge impact on my family, especially my father who fought in it when he was only 15 years old. He helped the U.S. soldiers fight against the communists, which eventually led to him being promised permanent residency in the United States. After the war, in 1985, he came to the U.S. following a difficult journey that included building a ship to escape Vietnam. The war shaped his life and ours, and it’s part of why we hold onto our Vietnamese culture so strongly today.

The United States became my father's new home after he left Vietnam in 1985 following the Vietnam War. He was promised permanent residency for helping U.S. soldiers during the war, and he eventually came here alone after building a ship to escape. Growing up in the U.S., I’ve tried to maintain my Vietnamese culture through cooking traditional dishes my father taught me. Now, as a family living in the United States, we blend Vietnamese and Nicaraguan cultures, sharing our heritage with our children in this diverse country.

I am married to a man from Nicaragua, and together we share our cultures with our children. While I teach them Vietnamese cuisine and traditions passed down from my father, my husband shares his Nicaraguan heritage. We split our meals during the week between Vietnamese and Nicaraguan food, which helps us maintain both cultures in our family life. This blending of Nicaragua and Vietnam in our household is a beautiful way to honor both our backgrounds and pass them on to the next generation.
